video_title: Why Did the Iran War Restart? | Macro Mondays w/ Andreas Steno & Mikkel Rosenvold
gist: On Real Vision's Macro Mondays, Andreas Steno tells host Mikkel Rosenvold that renewed Iran-US tension over the Strait of Hormuz is disrupting refined-product flows (not crude) more than headlines suggest, that US CPI should print well below consensus, and that both the memory/semiconductor trade and the US-China critical-metals decoupling trade are far from over despite vocal bearish calls.

insights.claim: Following renewed IRGC attacks and a stalled ceasefire, Donald Trump signaled the US wants to control or 'run' the Strait of Hormuz, an operationally undefined move (patrolling coastlines, seizing islands, or a naval blockade) that Andreas Steno says signals further disruption risk to oil flow even though its exact form is unclear.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: An ambiguous US military posture around Hormuz is a live tail-risk for oil and shipping insurance markets even without a formal closure.

insights.claim: Despite the escalation, spot oil prices have not even broken above the prior Thursday's peak, and tanker-tracking data (credited to analyst Rory Johnston) shows persistent 'dark transit' flows out of the Strait of Hormuz, much of it quietly routed through Abu Dhabi/the UAE, that Trump has not yet publicly acknowledged.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Real physical oil flow is materially better than the geopolitical headlines imply, arguing against chasing oil-price spikes on Hormuz fear alone.
insights.quote: it's been clear to me for a while that the flow was better than reported everywhere basically

insights.claim: The alternate oil transit route through Oman that had been used to bypass a closed Strait of Hormuz has now effectively been shut down after IRGC strikes over the weekend, removing one of the workarounds that had kept oil moving.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Loss of the Oman workaround narrows the remaining logistics options for oil exporters and raises the odds of further price volatility if other routes are targeted next.

insights.claim: Steno estimates the region is currently short about 4 million barrels/day versus a normalized oil flow, down from a peak shortfall of roughly 8 million b/d in April-May, with the US and China jointly bridging the gap via strategic petroleum reserve drawdowns that have only 3-5 months of remaining runway.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: The reserve-drawdown buffer is finite; watch for renewed oil-price pressure in roughly a quarter if the underlying shortfall isn't resolved by then.
insights.quote: my best assessment is that we lack four million barrels a day

insights.claim: Steno's base case is that oil settles around an $80 equilibrium and does not retest $100, arguing that once oil nears $100 the world 'gets very creative' (demand destruction, substitution, alternate routes) in ways not currently priced by the market.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: A capped ~$80 oil view argues against positioning for a runaway energy-driven inflation spike from the Iran conflict.
insights.quote: I think the equilibrium right now is probably 80

insights.claim: The real bottleneck from the Strait of Hormuz disruption is refined products, not crude — refiners have shifted marginal capacity toward jet fuel at the expense of gasoline and diesel, producing record-high US crack spreads and preventing the drop in crude from fully passing through to consumer fuel prices.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Crack-spread strength (rather than crude itself) is the metric to watch for when pump prices and near-term consumer inflation will actually ease.
insights.quote: we're talking record high crack spreads in the US because refiners have moved marginal capacity to jet fuel instead of um gasoline and diesel, etc.

insights.claim: China retains real leverage over oil prices heading into the US midterms because it could deliberately ramp oil purchases to move prices higher around the election, a risk Steno says the White House does not appear to be publicly addressing.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Election-cycle politics gives China an underappreciated lever over US energy costs, a geopolitical risk factor for oil positioning into next year's midterms.
insights.quote: the Chinese, they hold a pretty decent card if they start buying a lot of oil just before the midterms

insights.claim: Steno's proprietary now-casting model, run across every subcategory of the US CPI basket, points to a soft June US CPI print landing about 0.2 percentage points below consensus, driven by broad-based disinflation rather than primarily an energy-price story.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: A broadly soft CPI print (not just energy-driven) would be a stronger signal for the Fed to ease than an energy-only beat, supporting front-end rate repricing.
insights.quote: my best guess is that we get an a report that prints 0.2 percentage points below the consensus tomorrow

insights.claim: Steno's specific numeric call for the upcoming US CPI print is 3.7% headline (versus roughly 3.9% consensus and a 4.2% prior print) and 2.7% core inflation.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: A concrete, checkable inflation call investors can mark against the actual print to gauge Steno's now-casting track record and reposition the front end of the curve accordingly.
insights.quote: Three 3.7 for for headline and and 2.7 for for core.

insights.claim: The prior week's FOMC minutes were the most hawkish Federal Reserve communication since the 2022 hiking cycle, but Steno frames this as a forecasting error rather than a genuine change in the Fed's reaction function — meaning a soft CPI print should quickly cause the committee to reverse its hawkish tone.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: If the Fed's hawkishness reflects a bad inflation forecast rather than a fixed policy stance, a soft CPI print becomes a high-probability catalyst for rapid front-end rate repricing.
insights.quote: I see it more as a as a forecasting error than a policy error

insights.claim: The renewed Iran/Hormuz oil disruption may hand incoming Fed voice Kevin Warsh a convenient excuse to delay the start of a rate-cutting cycle, even though Steno expects political pressure on Warsh to cut eventually.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Geopolitical noise from Iran could delay a Fed pivot that the underlying inflation data would otherwise justify, a timing risk for rate-sensitive trades.

insights.claim: European June inflation data already came in benign outside of the energy component, reinforcing the view that the broader disinflation trend is not simply an oil-price story.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Confirms the disinflation narrative is broad-based across regions, supporting a lower-for-longer inflation view independent of oil headlines.

insights.claim: Steno dismisses the popular 'mean reversion' call on semiconductor and memory stocks associated with a vocal bearish crowd (including Michael Burry), arguing the trivial point that cyclicals shouldn't be bought at peak earnings misses the real question — whether the sector has actually peaked — and he sees no evidence of a peak in underlying spot prices.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Steno explicitly frames the bear case as lazy and unsupported by spot-price data, arguing traders shouldn't exit memory/semis on mean-reversion narratives alone.
insights.quote: I consider that analysis lazy to be honest.

insights.claim: Underlying spot prices for semiconductors/memory chips are still accelerating, up roughly another 20% since Micron and SK Hynix's latest updates, and a smaller Taiwanese chip supplier said last week it expects major players to hike prices 30-40% in Q3 — a move not yet reflected in forward earnings estimates.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Forward consensus earnings estimates likely understate the memory cycle's strength if a further 30-40% price hike lands in Q3, implying room for earnings beats.
insights.quote: we're probably up another 20% or so

insights.claim: There is a large divergence between bearish portfolio managers, who expect the semiconductor cycle to peak around Q1 2027, and the chipmakers themselves — SK Hynix says its order book is solid through 2031 and expects 2027 to be even wilder than 2026 — leading Steno to guess the cycle peaks closer to late 2027 or early 2028.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Company order-book guidance implies the memory upcycle extends roughly a year beyond current sell-side peak-earnings assumptions, favoring holding through near-term volatility.
insights.quote: they say we we basically have a solid orders book until 2031

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are directing effectively all of their free cash flow into semiconductor purchases, a capex transfer from hyperscalers to chipmakers that Steno calls the defining market story of 2026 (following 2025) and expects to continue into 2027.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Frames AI-infrastructure capex as a multi-year structural flow into chipmakers rather than a peaking trend, a lens for evaluating hyperscaler and semiconductor earnings through 2027.

insights.claim: South Korean export data, a high-frequency proxy for the memory trade, softened slightly in the first 10 days of July versus an 'extraordinary' June, but Steno attributes this to seasonal vacation effects rather than a genuine rollover, and flags it as the single most important chart to watch for the trade's end.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: South Korean export growth is the trigger to watch — a genuine rollover in this series (not just a seasonal dip) would be the signal to exit the memory trade.
insights.quote: when this chart rolls over um the memory trade is done

insights.claim: Steno discloses he personally holds about 9-10% of his portfolio in memory stocks and over 25% including broader semiconductor names, despite acknowledging his momentum-driven book has lost money over the prior two weeks.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: A concrete, transparent position size signals genuine high conviction in the memory trade despite recent short-term drawdown, useful context for weighing his bullish calls.
insights.quote: I have 9 10% of my portfolio in memory stocks

insights.claim: Key near-term catalysts for the memory/AI trade are bank earnings (Citigroup, Goldman Sachs) alongside US CPI on Tuesday, and ASML's earnings on Wednesday, which Steno calls the only real European gauge of AI capex momentum and an important global bellwether given the scrutiny over its China sales exposure.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: ASML's earnings and order book on Wednesday are a near-term binary catalyst for the AI capex narrative that investors in the memory/semis trade should watch closely.
insights.quote: ASML is yeah probably the only gauge you have in in Europe on on the AI momentum

insights.claim: Despite a recent Trump-Xi meeting in Beijing and talk of a trade 'ceasefire,' Chinese export data on critical/strategic metals (through June 1) shows no reversal of the underlying decoupling trend, even though some of Steno's decoupling-themed portfolio positions suffered short-term price hits from the ceasefire narrative.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: The ceasefire narrative that pressured decoupling-themed metals positions is not supported by actual trade-flow data, arguing for staying with the decoupling trade rather than exiting on headline risk.
insights.quote: the trade relationship is as frosty as it's ever been

insights.claim: Steno expects any critical-metals scarcity risk to be mitigated the way Russia sanctions were circumvented — China rerouting sales through third countries such as Malaysia that maintain good relations with the US — meaning an outright supply cutoff is unlikely even if tensions escalate further.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Third-party rerouting caps the tail risk of an actual critical-metals supply shock, tempering how aggressively to price in scarcity premiums.

insights.claim: The long-term US-China critical-metals decoupling trade remains intact and is likely to see major investment regardless of the outcome of upcoming US and EU elections, with the EU now adopting what Steno calls a version of 'the Trump playbook' to secure its own supply chain independence from China.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: Supply-chain reshoring/derisking from China is a durable multi-year theme in both the US and EU regardless of election outcomes, supporting a long-horizon allocation to critical-metals and reshoring plays.

insights.claim: Steno argues the metals-decoupling trade won't gain price momentum until the US dollar weakens and energy costs fall, which in turn requires a softer inflation print and a shift in Federal Reserve rhetoric; he has begun building exposure but remains deliberately cautious until the Fed acknowledges the disinflation his data already shows.
insights.speaker: Andreas Steno
insights.so_what: The critical-metals trade's timing is gated by the dollar and Fed rhetoric, not fundamentals alone — a softer CPI print and dovish Fed pivot are the triggers to watch before adding size.
insights.quote: our data is screaming it in one direction right inflation down um softer dollar everything related to that but the those in charge they haven't really acknowledged that
